topic: Error Handling - Box<dyn Error> vs Typed Errors

turns.content: I'm trying to figure out the best way to handle errors in Rust, especially for my performance-critical game code. I see all these fancy traits like Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync + 'static> everywhere, but why does it even work this way? What's really going on?

Well, imagine you're writing a Rust library for your game, and you want errors to be thread-safe and portable across different parts of the codebase. Wrapping errors in `Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync + 'static>` is like putting a universal wrapper on top of any error type—so it can travel anywhere safely without knowing exactly what kind of error it is.
Hmm, but why do I need all those traits? Can't just use `Box<dyn Error>`?

`Send + Sync` are important because they let the error cross thread boundaries—say from one background task to another. And `'static` means it doesn't borrow anything, making it safe to store in a shared container or pass between threads without worrying about lifetimes.
So, wrapping errors is good for portability? But what if I want detailed error info?

Exactly. You might have a custom error type with lots of details (like `thiserror` or `anyhow`). Wrapping them in `Box<dyn Error>` means you can still get those details later when you need to, but sometimes that's overkill if you just want something simple and portable.
So should I always use boxed errors then?

No. If your error types are small and have clear meaning, keeping them unboxed can be cleaner—like with `thiserror` macros that derive `Error`. But if you're writing a library or want portable code across threads, `Box<dyn Error>` is the safer bet.
Okay, but what about recovering from those boxed errors? How do I check if it's a specific error?

Good question. You can use `downcast_ref` (from the `std::error::Error` trait) to try and cast your boxed error back into its original type. If it matches, you get access to the details; if not, you know something else happened.
Ah, I see—so sometimes you want portability (`Box<dyn Error>`), and sometimes you need precise error info (typed errors). That makes sense. But wait, does that mean I should keep a mix of both?
No need to do that. You can design your API so it returns `Box<dyn Error>` by default for portability, but internally you keep typed errors if you want detailed recovery.
Exactly! So I should stick with `Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync + 'static>` most of the time, but be ready to downcast when needed?

Yes. That's a great strategy. It balances portability and precise error handling. You'll write cleaner code with typed errors internally, but give your API that universal boxed trait for safe use elsewhere.

topic: Error Handling - Box<dyn Error> vs Typed Errors

turns.content: I'm reviewing our new error handling proposal and I'm confused about why we're using Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync + 'static>' everywhere instead of concrete types.

The main reason for that specific trait bound is thread safety. When you write Box<dyn Error>, the inner type must implement all those traits so multiple threads can safely share or send it across boundaries without needing to know the exact error type.
So I don't have to check every possible error variant in my code, which makes things simpler. But what happens if I actually need to get details from a specific error later?

You can use downcast_ref to recover the original type if you really need it, but this is expensive because you have to manually match on the inner boxed value instead of letting Rust's compiler do the work for you.
Wait, I thought downcast_ref just returned me the concrete type automatically without any cost. That sounds wrong and I need to check if this is actually true or a myth.

That's a common misconception. Downcast_ref doesn't magically give you free access; it requires explicit pattern matching on the Box<dyn Error> to extract the original type, which means your code becomes more verbose and less safe if you don't handle all cases correctly.

Okay so I should always keep typed errors in my library unless performance is critical. But what about when we need to send these errors between different modules that might have different error definitions?

When you have heterogeneous error types across boundaries, the Box<dyn Error + Send + Sync + 'static>' approach lets you wrap them uniformly. This works well for internal APIs where you want to avoid compile-time errors from mismatched types.
That makes sense but I'm worried about the runtime overhead of wrapping every error in a Box instead of just using references or direct ownership. Is that really worth it?

The Box adds heap allocation which costs more than stack references, but the benefit is avoiding the compile-time errors from mismatched types and enabling safe sharing across threads without needing to know the exact error type at compile time.
